new_characters.name: Hadji Murad
new_characters.initial_description: Shamil's former naib, legendary fighter, now a fugitive travelling in disguise with only one murid; limps slightly; rides into a hostile Chechen aoul seeking shelter and a courier to the Russians.

new_characters.name: Eldar
new_characters.initial_description: Hadji Murad's sole murid companion; young, strong, silent — sits statue-still while women are present; mirrors his leader's every gesture.

new_characters.name: Sado
new_characters.initial_description: Master of the house in Makhmet aoul; about forty, small beard, long nose; receives Hadji Murad as kunak despite Shamil's death-penalty order; embodies Chechen hospitality code.

new_characters.name: Old Man (Apiarist)
new_characters.initial_description: Elderly Chechen, Sado's relative; moist red lashless eyes; lives at an apiary; delivers veiled news about Shamil's order in proverbial language ('the hares and the eagles').

new_characters.name: Sado's Son
new_characters.initial_description: Boy of about fifteen, sloe-black eyes; runs to call his father; gazes at Hadji Murad throughout with wide-eyed admiration.

new_characters.name: Bata
new_characters.initial_description: Short-legged, sun-burnt Chechen guide; boastful but capable; agrees to escort Eldar (or Khan Mahoma's murid) to Russian commander Vorontsov in exchange for three coins — though he claims honour is enough.

new_characters.name: Sado's Wife
new_characters.initial_description: Thin middle-aged woman in yellow smock and red beshmet; arranges cushions and brings food; silent, deferential.

new_characters.name: Sado's Daughter
new_characters.initial_description: Young girl in red trousers and green beshmet; silver coin necklace; tries to look stern; does not look at male guests — traditional Chechen modesty.

new_characters.name: Khan Mahoma
new_characters.initial_description: One of Hadji Murad's four murids waiting at the Argun glade; entrusted with delivering Hadji Murad's message to Prince Vorontsov; appears only by name in this chunk.

new_characters.name: Author-Narrator (I)
new_characters.initial_description: First-person frame narrator; a traveller who discovers the battered thistle on a ploughed field and uses it as the trigger to 'remember' the Hadji Murad episode.

plot_elements.description: Author-narrator encounters a crushed but upright thistle on a ploughed field — its defiant survival triggers the memory of Hadji Murad. Establishes the central symbol and narrative frame.
Hadji Murad rides into Makhmet disguised and accompanied by only one murid (Eldar), signalling that he has broken from Shamil and is now a fugitive.
Hadji Murad seeks shelter at Sado's house; old man delivers news that Shamil has issued a kill-or-capture order on Hadji Murad to all Chechens.
Sado accepts Hadji Murad as kunak despite Shamil's death-penalty edict — dramatises the conflict between the Chechen hospitality code (adat) and Shamil's religious-political authority (sharia-state).
Hadji Murad dispatches Bata as guide to lead Khan Mahoma and his murids through Argun forest to Russian commander Prince Vorontsov — the envoy mission is set in motion.
Hadji Murad passes a note hidden in a cartridge pouch for Sado to forward to his son — first signal that his family is a driver behind his defection.
Old man's proverb: 'the hares keep discussing how to drive away the eagles' — veiled commentary on Chechen resistance; Russian raid on Mitchit aoul mentioned.

new_locations.name: Makhmet Aoul
new_locations.description: Hostile Chechen village ~15 miles from Russian territory; smoky with burning kizyak; densely packed saklyas on a hillside; a fountain below where women and children gather.

new_locations.name: Sado's Saklya
new_locations.description: Two-room stone/clay house cut into the hillside; penthouse entrance; weapons hung on whitewashed walls; earthen floor; intimate, low-ceilinged domestic space. Core scene of the kunak hospitality sequence.

new_locations.name: Argun River Glade
new_locations.description: Forest clearing by a cliff-bend of the Argun river where two haystacks stand; rendezvous point for Hadji Murad's four waiting murids.

new_locations.name: Mitchit Aoul
new_locations.description: Nearby Chechen aoul mentioned by old man as having been burnt by Russian soldiers — background event establishing Russian aggression.

new_locations.name: Ploughed Field (Narrator's Walk)
new_locations.description: Large black tilled field on a hill near which the narrator finds the thistle; opening frame location, agricultural and desolate.

theme_hints: Vitality under oppression — the thistle as embodied theme
Hospitality vs. political survival (kunak code vs. Shamil's edict)
Defection and betrayal as political acts with personal stakes
The individual crushed by imperial/theocratic machinery yet refusing to yield
Silence as resistance — Hadji Murad's economy of speech mirrors self-preservation
symbols: The Tartar thistle — compressed, broken, yet upright; direct visual analogue of Hadji Murad
The cartridge pouch hiding the letter — concealment of personal love beneath military armour
Weapons hung side by side on Sado's wall — equality of guest and host; Chechen code materialised
Honey — abundance, goodness, domestic peace set against violence outside
notes: This chunk is the narrative hinge: the thistle-frame prologue (authorial digression, Chapter-I equivalent) transitions into the story proper. Chunk covers approximately Chapters I–II of the novel. Shamil's kill-order is already active, meaning the defection has already occurred before this chunk begins — Chunk 1 likely established the wider context. The kunak relationship with Sado is load-bearing (must-keep thread). Bata is an efficient single-use operative. The narrator's 'I' exists only in the prologue and can be dissolved entirely in film — the thistle can open the film as a pure visual image without voice-over.

fusion_candidates.reason: Bata's sole function is to guide the murids to the Russian lines; Khan Mahoma's sole function is to be guided there. In a film these two can be collapsed into one character — a murid-envoy who both knows the terrain and carries the message — eliminating one named walk-on.

The old man delivers the exposition (Shamil's order, burned Mitchit aoul) and then Sado repeats and expands it. In a compressed screenplay Sado alone can carry all this information; the old man is a relay character whose lines fold into the host's opening speech.

Both women appear only to serve food and embody Chechen domestic space. They share no dialogue and can be merged into a single silent female figure without narrative loss. Pre-identified fusion candidate confirmed.

cuttable.element: Author-Narrator's thistle monologue (inner thought about man's destructiveness)
cuttable.reason: The interior philosophical reflection ('How many different plant-lives...') is a classic Tolstoyan authorial digression. The film equivalent is a pure visual: close-up of the crushed, upright thistle — no voice-over needed. The reflex to Hadji Murad can be established by cutting directly from the thistle to his face. The verbal meditation is non-load-bearing.
Background world-building delivered by the old man. The fact of Russian raids is already established by other means (the defection context). This line is cuttable without information loss.

cuttable.element: Old Man's ritualistic descent from roof (extended physical business)
cuttable.reason: Detailed novelistic staging (blinked eyes, wooden-heeled slippers, shrivelled neck, mumbling) is literary texture, not dramaturgical necessity. A film can show him in two shots.

cuttable.element: Bata's self-promotional speech ('There is not another Chechen who would pass as I can...')
cuttable.reason: Comic character texture. Functional information (he can guide) lands in one line. The boast is cuttable without plot loss.

cuttable.element: Sado's Son as named recurring presence
cuttable.reason: The boy appears three times (running, returning with Bata, sitting by door). He is mood and colour, not plot. Can be cut or reduced to one establishing beat.

refocalize.scene: Thistle prologue — narrator walks the ploughed field, discovers the thistle, reflects philosophically
refocalize.recommended_pov: Remove POV entirely — open on EXTREME CLOSE-UP of the broken-but-upright thistle, then pull back to reveal the empty black field. No narrator. The image is self-sufficient. Cut to: Hadji Murad's face in motion.

refocalize.scene: Old man's arrival — descends from roof, tells news about Shamil's order in proverb-form
refocalize.current_pov: Close third-person following the old man's physical movements
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's POV — he watches and reads the old man. The old man's hesitation and oblique speech register as threat-assessment from HM's perspective rather than as novelistic character sketch.

refocalize.scene: Women serve food — Sado's wife and daughter move through the room, men go silent
refocalize.current_pov: Neutral third-person observing the domestic ritual
refocalize.recommended_pov: Eldar's tightly controlled stillness (the novel explicitly follows his gaze being suppressed) — a brief insert shot of his clenched hands, then relief when the door closes. More cinematic, preserves the cultural note without stopping the scene.
